title: Ferromagnetism from the geometry of localized wavefunctions in moiré systems
abstract: We present a mechanism for ferromagnetism in narrow bands consisting of Anderson-localized states. We exploit single-particle localization to derive a controlled theory of exchange interactions within the narrow band. For quasiperiodic systems with a half-filled moiré band, we show that the critical interaction strength for ferromagnetism is highly sensitive to the geometry of real-space overlaps between localized orbitals: we find well-defined resonances at which ferromagnetism sets in for interaction energies that are far lower than the gap to other bands. Near these resonances, all the approximations in our theory are controlled, so our critical point predictions are quantitative. We show examples both in one and two dimensions. Our work identifies a route to ferromagnetism based on the geometry of real-space wavefunctions, distinct from previously found mechanisms based on the quantum geometry of Bloch bands.
summary: The paper develops a theory to explain ferromagnetism in narrow bands of localized electrons found in moiré superlattices. It shows that the magnetic stability depends critically on the geometric overlap of single-particle wavefunctions rather than just band curvature. By analyzing spin excitations, the authors establish quantitative predictions for the critical interaction strength required to induce magnetism.
main_problem: The research aims to determine the mechanism for ferromagnetism in narrow electronic bands formed by Anderson-localized states within moiré superlattices.
main_result: Ferromagnetism can be stabilized at critical interaction strengths ($U_c$) determined by the geometry of real-space overlaps between localized wavefunctions, providing a route distinct from traditional quantum geometry arguments.
method: The analysis involves projecting the Hubbard model onto the narrow band subspace and studying the stability of the fully polarized state by examining spin-flip excitations (magnons).
model_or_system: The system is modeled using an effective Hamiltonian for quasiperiodic moiré lattices, incorporating nearest-neighbor hopping and on-site Coulomb repulsion ($U$). The physics is analyzed in terms of localized single-particle eigenstates.
key_observables: Critical interaction strength ($U_c$), magnon excitation spectrum, Inverse Participation Ratio (IPR), and the energy difference between spin sectors ($\Delta E$).
important_parameters: Interaction strength ($U$), hopping parameters ($t$, $V$), localization length ($\xi$), and wavefunction overlap geometry ($ho_{\alpha eta}$).
main_assumptions: The theory relies on projecting the full Hamiltonian onto a narrow-band subspace, assuming that low-energy physics is dominated by these localized states.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the evolution of the magnon spectrum (transition from continuum to bound modes) and show how $U_c$ depends on system parameters, confirming robustness checks across varying numbers of included high-energy states.
paper_structure: The paper systematically derives effective Hamiltonians for charge and spin excitations, calculates single-particle properties like IPR using locator expansions, and finally uses exact diagonalization to verify the global ground state transition point ($U_c$) by comparing low-spin vs. fully polarized energy sectors.
why_interesting: This work provides a concrete, geometrically driven mechanism for magnetism in correlated systems without translational symmetry, which is highly relevant for understanding emergent phenomena in moiré heterostructures and artificial quantum materials.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Problem and main results. (a) Illustration of a quasi- periodic potential (points in gray) forming a moiré pattern with length ℓ. In the localized phase, the eigenstates in the lowest-energy narrow band are localized around the potential minima in each moiré cell. (b) Non-interacting spectrum of the 1D Aubry-André model, colored by the inverse participa- tion ratio (IPR). We focus on half filling of the low-energy nar- row band highlighted by the dashed box, within the localized phase. The bandwidth W is much smaller than the gap ∆G to higher-energy states, allowing a controlled narrow-band pro- jection of the Hamiltonian when the relevant interaction scale satisfies U ≪∆G. (c)...

FIG. 2. Numerical and analytical results for charge excita- tions for the 1D Aubry-André model with t2 = t3 = 0. (a) Interacting contribution to the spectrum of charge excita- tions by diagonalization of the U-dependent term in Eq. 4, as a function of the eigenstate label ν = α/NB, where α = 0, · · · , NB −1. The analytical results using the inter- acting term in Eq. 5 are also shown. (b) Charge gap ∆c as a function of V for U = 3/4. The insets illustrate the dominant particle-hole excitations for small (red) and large (blue) V . The analytical expressions are provided in the SM.

FIG. 3. Spin-flip excitations and magnons. (a) Example magnon spectrum for V = 3.5, t2 = −0.7 and L = 225. The upper insets illustrate different competing excitations in the particle-hole continuum. The lower inset shows a close- up of the narrow magnon bound-state branch. For U > U ∗

FIG. 4. Ferromagnets induced by wavefunction detuning. Top panels: Uc as a function of t2 for different system sizes. Bottom panels: squared overlap norm ∥ραβ∥2 and wavefunc- tion detuning D2 αβ for the dominant nearest-neighbor bonds entering the estimate of Uc in Eq. (11) (shown in green). We restrict to pairs with ∆αβ > δr = 0.01, for which the off- diagonal particle-hole states |α, β⟩can be reliably integrated out. We also show the single-particle gap ∆G, the narrow- band bandwidth W, and the average inverse participation ra- tio ⟨IPR⟩of the narrow-band eigenstates. (a) Results for the 1D model with t3 = 0. (b) Results for the 2D model with t2 = −1.25t3.
